title: From curl to Code
subtitle: Every curl command translates directly into real code

sections.content:
Here's the secret: `curl` isn't just a testing tool. It's a **translation guide**.

Every curl command you write maps 1:1 to a fetch call in JavaScript, a requests call in Python, or any other HTTP client. Once a request works in curl, translating it to code is mechanical — and Claude Code does it in seconds.

This is the workflow every developer uses:
1. Understand the API (read docs)
2. Test the request with curl (confirm it works)
3. Translate to code (copy into your project)
4. Handle the response (parse and use the data)
keyPoints: curl flags map directly to code: `-H` → headers object, `-d` → body
Any working curl command can be pasted to Claude Code: "translate this to JavaScript fetch"
The translation is mechanical — the hard part is getting the curl working, not the code
This workflow works for every language: JS, Python, Go, Ruby, etc.

sections.instruction: The same request in curl, JavaScript (fetch), and Python (requests).

blocks.label: JavaScript (fetch)
Python (requests)
blocks.code:
import requests

response = requests.get(
    'https://api.github.com/users/torvalds',
    headers={'Accept': 'application/json'}
)
data = response.json()
print(data['name'])  # Linus Torvalds

sections.explanation:
Notice the mapping:
- URL → first argument
- `-H "Key: Value"` → headers object `{ 'Key': 'Value' }`
- `-X POST` → `method: 'POST'`
- `-d '{...}'` → `body: JSON.stringify({...})`

The structure is identical. The syntax is different.

sections.instruction: A POST request — the same data sent three ways.

blocks.label: curl
blocks.code:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/posts \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer MY_TOKEN" \
  -d '{"title": "Hello", "body": "World"}'

blocks.label: JavaScript (fetch)
Python (requests)

sections.instruction: Write a curl command to get the 3 most recently updated public repos for `microsoft` on GitHub. Then ask Claude Code to translate it to JavaScript.
sections.expectedOutput: An array of 3 repo objects. Confirm it works, then copy the command.

sections.instruction: Once your curl works, use this prompt with Claude Code to translate it to code.
sections.prompt:
Translate this curl command into a JavaScript async function that returns the parsed JSON:

curl "https://api.github.com/users/microsoft/repos?sort=updated&per_page=3"

The function should be called `getMicrosoftRepos` and handle errors gracefully.

placeholders.description: Paste your working curl command here

sections.expectedResult: An async JavaScript function that uses fetch, handles errors, and returns the array of repos.

completionMessage:
Level 4B Complete: Talk to the Internet

You can now:
  ✓ Read and construct any URL
  ✓ Make GET, POST requests with curl
  ✓ Add headers and authentication
  ✓ Use query parameters to filter results
  ✓ Read API documentation and translate it
  ✓ Diagnose HTTP errors systematically
  ✓ Translate curl commands to code

The internet is just requests and responses. You now speak the language.

Next up: Level 5 — Building With Real Tools
You'll build the server that answers these requests.

milestone.title: Level 4B Complete: Talk to the Internet
summary: You can construct and read any URL
You make real HTTP requests from the terminal using curl
You authenticate with API keys and request headers
You read API documentation and translate it into working commands
You diagnose HTTP errors by status code and response body
You know how curl maps directly to JavaScript and Python code
milestone.nextLevelTeaser: In Level 5, you'll flip to the other side — you'll BUILD the server that answers these requests.
